title: Abelian varieties with maximal Galois action on their torsion points
speaker: David Zywina
abstract: Abstract: Associated to an abelian variety A/K is a Galois representation which describes the action of the absolute Galois group of K on the torsion points of A. In this talk, we shall describe how large the image of this representation can be (in terms of a number field K and the dimension of A). We achieve this by considering abelian varieties in families and then using a special variant of Hilbert's irreducibility theorem. Some results of Serre on the mod ell Galois image will also be reviewed. (This is joint work with David Zureick-Brown)

proof_stages.label: Torsion Galois representation and Weil-pairing bound
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a number field and a principally polarized abelian variety, packages the Galois action on all torsion points into a profinite representation, and identifies the Weil pairing as the universal visible constraint forcing the image into a group of symplectic similitudes with cyclotomic multiplier.

proof_stages.label: Maximal-image goal via families and specialization
proof_stages.summary: The first maximality theorem is stated and then reframed by passing from a single abelian variety to a family over a rational base, where étale fundamental-group monodromy gives a geometric upper bound and Hilbert irreducibility suggests that most specializations attain it.

proof_stages.label: Big-monodromy density theorem away from Q
proof_stages.summary: Under a finite-index geometric monodromy assumption, the main density theorem asserts that for k not equal to Q, most k-points of the base specialize to abelian varieties whose torsion Galois image is exactly the largest image allowed by the Weil-pairing multiplier.

proof_stages.label: Kronecker-Weber obstruction over Q
proof_stages.summary: For k=Q, the cyclotomic extension is the maximal abelian extension, so the commutator subgroup imposes an additional compatibility condition, leading to a theorem that the generic image has a fixed index rather than being the full expected group.

proof_stages.label: Small-prime exceptional set by explicit Hilbert irreducibility
proof_stages.summary: The proof is split prime-by-prime, and for primes below a logarithmic height-dependent cutoff, explicit Hilbert irreducibility and sieve bounds show that the proportion of specializations with too-small mod-l image tends to zero.

proof_stages.label: Large-prime control by effective Serre theory
proof_stages.summary: For primes above the cutoff, an effective version of Serre’s theory constructs reductive subgroups over finite fields whose points uniformly approximate the mod-l Galois images, with constants controlled by the dimension, the number field, and Faltings height.

proof_stages.label: Rank identification and assembly of maximality
proof_stages.summary: The reductive groups arising for large l are identified by rank as the expected symplectic similitude groups, and together with the small-prime sieve and independence arguments this yields the claimed generic maximal Galois image.

examples.name: Complex-torus picture of torsion
examples.note: Thinking of the complex points of an abelian variety as a torus illustrates why the abstract m-torsion group is just a rank-2g module over Z/mZ.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the complex-torus picture to read off the concrete structure of the m-torsion group as a rank 2g module over Z/mZ.

examples.name: Elliptic curves over Q
examples.note: Elliptic curves, especially the Weierstrass family y^2=x^3+ax+b, illustrate the special Q-obstruction by showing that the expected image is typically of index 2 rather than full.
examples.engagement_reason: Elliptic curves over Q are invoked through known results and the usual Weierstrass family, but no computation or derivation is carried out on them.

passes.reason: Elliptic curves over Q are cited in the small-dimension Q case where the naive full adelic image statement fails by the Serre/Kronecker-Weber obstruction.

examples.name: Hyperelliptic family y^2=f(x)
examples.note: The family of hyperelliptic curves with separable polynomial f illustrates how a rational parameter space yields fibers whose Jacobians can be counted by height and tested for large Galois image.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the hyperelliptic family via separable polynomials, describes its parameter space, and analyzes its monodromy and mod-2 image in terms of Weierstrass points.

examples.name: Generic hyperelliptic curve and Manin-Mumford torsion intersection
examples.note: A hyperelliptic curve embedded in its Jacobian via the point at infinity illustrates an arithmetic-geometric consequence of maximal Galois image: generically its torsion intersection is just the Weierstrass points.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the generic Galois-image result to a hyperelliptic curve in its Jacobian and identifies its torsion intersection as the Weierstrass points.

passes.reason: The generic hyperelliptic curve is used to illustrate an application computing the Manin-Mumford torsion intersection as the Weierstrass points.
